3.3.a.info:
The IPv4 default route is the catch-all route a router uses when no more specific destination matches in the routing table. Its syntax is ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop | exit-interface]. The destination 0.0.0.0 with mask 0.0.0.0 means 'match zero bits of the destination' — which mathematically matches every possible IPv4 address. Because its prefix length is /0, it is the LEAST specific route possible and always loses longest prefix match against any more specific route.

Where it's used: Default routes are most common on edge routers that connect the enterprise network to the internet or to a single upstream provider. A branch office router typically has one default route pointing at the HQ or ISP router — it doesn't need to know every internet prefix because there's only one exit path. Core routers in larger networks often learn the default dynamically via OSPF's default-information originate or BGP.

Configuration examples: ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1 (recursive, pointing at next-hop IP), ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 GigabitEthernet0/0 (directly attached, exit interface only — only appropriate on point-to-point links), or ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 GigabitEthernet0/0 203.0.113.1 (fully specified, best practice on Ethernet).

Verification: When a default route is active, show ip route displays Gateway of last resort is 203.0.113.1 to network 0.0.0.0 at the top. The route itself appears as S* 0.0.0.0/0 [1/0] via 203.0.113.1 — the asterisk (*) marks it as a candidate default route. Without a default, the table reads 'Gateway of last resort is not set' and packets to unknown destinations are dropped with ICMP Destination Unreachable.

Common mistake: Typing the mask as 255.255.255.255 (a /32 host route matching only 0.0.0.0) instead of 0.0.0.0 (a /0 catch-all). The command accepts both without error — but only 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 is a real default route. Muscle-memorize the all-zeros on both halves.

hack.memory: 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 = 'I don't care what the destination is — just ship it.' The /0 mask matches every address but loses to any more specific route. S* in 'show ip route' means Static candidate default — the asterisk is your cue that this is the gateway of last resort.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: build R1 (branch) → R2 (ISP) → simulated internet host. On R1 run 'ping 8.8.8.8' — fails, no route. Add 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [R2-IP]'. Run 'show ip route' — note the 'Gateway of last resort' line at the top and the S* entry. Ping again — succeeds. Remove the default with 'no ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [R2-IP]' and watch pings fail instantly.
hack.meta: Near-guaranteed lab sim question on the CCNA. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 (Static Routing) drills the exact syntax. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Know the all-zeros pattern cold — exam will present half-configured routes and ask what's missing.

3.3.a.info:
The IPv6 default route is the catch-all for IPv6 just as 0.0.0.0/0 is for IPv4. Its prefix is ::/0 — the IPv6 unspecified address with a /0 mask, meaning zero bits must match so every IPv6 destination is a hit. Syntax: ipv6 route ::/0 [next-hop | exit-interface].

Why ::/0 instead of all zeros in dotted form: IPv6 uses CIDR prefix notation natively rather than a separate dotted-decimal mask. The double-colon (::) is the all-zeros address in compressed IPv6 form, and /0 is the prefix length. Writing it as 0:0:0:0:0:0:0:0/0 is legal but nobody does.

Three valid forms: ipv6 route ::/0 2001:DB8:FEED::1 (recursive global-unicast next-hop), ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/0 (exit interface only), and ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/0 2001:DB8:FEED::1 (fully specified). A special case that catches many students: when using a link-local next-hop like fe80::1, you MUST also give the exit interface because link-local addresses are only unique per-interface. Example: ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/0 fe80::1.

Prerequisite — ipv6 unicast-routing: Even with a perfect default route configured, the router will not forward any IPv6 packet until the global command ipv6 unicast-routing is enabled. Without it, the router processes IPv6 destined to itself but silently drops transit IPv6 traffic. This is a HUGE exam trap — a default route exists, addresses are configured, but pings fail because unicast-routing is off.

Verification: show ipv6 route displays the IPv6 routing table. A static default appears as S ::/0 [1/0] via 2001:DB8:FEED::1. show ipv6 route static filters to static routes only. Unlike IPv4, the IPv6 routing table does not display a 'gateway of last resort' line at the top — just look for the S entry with ::/0 prefix.

hack.memory: ::/0 is the IPv6 answer to 0.0.0.0/0. Double-colon = all zeros, /0 = match nothing (so match everything). If the next-hop starts with fe80:: you MUST append the exit interface — link-local is per-interface, not global. And NEVER forget 'ipv6 unicast-routing' globally, or no IPv6 traffic is forwarded at all.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: (1) Enable 'ipv6 unicast-routing' on R1. (2) Configure a global-unicast IPv6 on R1-R2 transit link and on R2's LAN. (3) Configure 'ipv6 route ::/0 [R2 global IPv6]' on R1. (4) Verify with 'show ipv6 route' — note the S ::/0 entry. (5) Replace the next-hop with R2's link-local (fe80::) WITHOUT the exit interface — observe the error. (6) Fix with 'ipv6 route ::/0 Gi0/0 fe80::2' and confirm it installs.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 31-33 cover IPv6 fundamentals and static routing. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 23-25. The exam loves IPv6 static-route questions — specifically the link-local + exit-interface rule and the 'ipv6 unicast-routing' gotcha. Memorize ::/0 syntax exactly; typos fail on sim questions.

3.3.a.info:
The branch office pattern is the textbook use case for a default static route. A branch site typically has a single WAN link to HQ or the ISP and therefore only one possible exit for all traffic heading off-site. In this topology there is nothing to gain from running a full dynamic routing protocol to learn the entire internet — a single static default route accomplishes the same goal with far less complexity and CPU.

Why it works: From the branch's perspective every non-local destination is 'somewhere out there, through the ISP.' Instead of maintaining a RIB with tens of thousands of BGP prefixes, the branch simply installs ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [ISP-next-hop] and relies on the upstream router to know the specifics. This is called stub routing — the branch is a dead-end 'stub' of the larger network.

Typical configuration: Branch router has connected routes for its local LAN(s), a default static toward the ISP/HQ, and potentially a few specific static routes if private WAN traffic takes a different path than internet traffic. The upstream (HQ or ISP) router in turn needs a route back to the branch's LAN prefix — either a static route or a prefix learned dynamically. Forgetting this reverse static on the HQ side is the most common cause of 'ping works from HQ side but not from branch LAN' failures.

Scaling signal: The moment a branch has TWO upstream paths (primary + backup WAN, or dual-homed to two ISPs) the single-default-route pattern stops being sufficient by itself. You either (a) add a floating static out the backup with higher AD, or (b) run a dynamic protocol like OSPF/BGP to let the router decide. The default route still exists — it's just no longer the only tool in the box.

Security angle: A default static route is also what you override when using policy-based routing (PBR) to force certain traffic (say, guest VLAN internet access) out a specific interface. The stub pattern keeps things simple until the network grows up.

hack.memory: Branch = dead-end street. Only one way out, so a single 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [upstream]' is all the branch needs. HQ still has to configure a specific return route pointing at the branch LAN — otherwise traffic goes out but never comes back. Stub topology = stub routing.
hack.practice: Build a hub-and-spoke in Packet Tracer: HQ router with three branches connected via WAN links. On each branch, configure ONLY 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [HQ-IP]' — no dynamic routing. On HQ, configure three specific static routes for each branch's LAN. Ping from every branch LAN to every other branch LAN. This lab cements WHY the pattern works and why the return route on HQ is mandatory.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 shows this exact pattern. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15 uses 'stub network' terminology. The exam tests this as a topology diagram question: 'what's the minimum routing config to make branch traffic reach the internet?' — the answer is one default on the branch plus a return route on HQ.

3.3.a.info:
In show ip route output the code S* marks a static candidate default route — a static route to 0.0.0.0/0 that is currently acting as the gateway of last resort. The S is the normal code for a static route; the asterisk (*) is the 'candidate default' marker that appears on any route eligible to be the default (0.0.0.0/0 entry from any source).

Why 'candidate': More than one protocol can inject a default. A static S* 0.0.0.0/0, an OSPF-originated O*E2 0.0.0.0/0, and a BGP B* 0.0.0.0/0 can all coexist in the RIB as candidates, but only ONE wins per-prefix (via AD + metric) and that winner is the actual gateway of last resort shown at the top of the routing table.

Reading the full entry: S* 0.0.0.0/0 [1/0] via 203.0.113.1. Breakdown: S = Static, * = candidate default, 0.0.0.0/0 = the prefix, [1/0] = [AD 1 / metric 0], via 203.0.113.1 = next-hop IP. The top of the table will also show Gateway of last resort is 203.0.113.1 to network 0.0.0.0.

When S* disappears: If the exit interface goes down, or the next-hop IP becomes unreachable, the static default is pulled from the RIB and the 'Gateway of last resort' line switches to 'not set.' This instantly turns every packet to an unknown destination into an ICMP Destination Unreachable. Monitoring for the absence of S* is a quick troubleshoot — if your branch lost internet, show ip route and check for S* first.

Exam relevance: You will absolutely see a show ip route snippet with S* and be asked to identify (a) that it's a static default, (b) what the next hop is, and (c) what the AD is. Get this recognition to single-glance speed.

right.label: Gateway of last resort
items: Printed at top of show ip route

hack.memory: S* = Static + star (candidate default). The asterisk is the eye-catch — whenever you see S* you're looking at the router's emergency exit. No S* and no equivalent (O*E2, B*) means 'gateway of last resort is not set' = unknown destinations get dropped.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: on a router with no default, run 'show ip route' and find 'Gateway of last resort is not set.' Add 'ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]' and re-run the command. Note the new top line AND the S* entry in the body. Now enable OSPF and inject a default via 'default-information originate' on another router — the entry changes to O*E2. Observe how the * marker follows whichever route is currently acting as default.
hack.meta: Pure recognition question on the exam. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Know every route-code letter and the '*' meaning — there are likely 2-3 questions that hinge on reading 'show ip route' output correctly.

3.3.b.info:
The network static route command is: ip route [destination-network] [subnet-mask] [next-hop-ip | exit-interface] [optional-AD]. This is the fundamental building block of static routing — it tells the router 'to reach network X with mask Y, forward packets via Z.' Every static route you ever configure is a variation of this pattern.

Breaking down the four pieces: (1) Destination network — the network prefix in dotted-decimal, e.g. 10.1.1.0. Must be a network address, not a host address (though IOS will silently correct host addresses to the proper network). (2) Subnet mask — dotted-decimal mask matching the prefix length, e.g. 255.255.255.0 for /24. Unlike OSPF/EIGRP, static routes use a real mask, not a wildcard. (3) Next-hop-IP OR exit-interface — either the IP of the next router or the local interface that leads toward the destination. (4) Optional AD — a number 1-255 appended at the end to override the default AD of 1 (used for floating static routes).

Concrete examples: ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2 (recursive next-hop), ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 Serial0/0/0 (exit interface — only appropriate on P2P), ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 GigabitEthernet0/0 192.168.1.2 (fully specified), ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.2.2 210 (floating static with AD 210).

Where the route shows up: Once active, the route appears in show ip route as S 10.1.1.0/24 [1/0] via 192.168.1.2. The S code indicates static, [1/0] is [AD/metric] — static routes always have metric 0. If the exit interface is down or the next-hop unreachable, the route will be in the running-config but NOT in the routing table. This is why 'show run' and 'show ip route' tell different stories.

Removing a route: Prepend no to the exact command used to configure it: no ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2. Partial commands may not match — use the full line to be safe.

params.label: Anatomy of a static route command

hack.memory: Four parts: destination network + mask + next-hop/interface + optional AD. The AD at the end is the single most-missed piece — forget it and your 'floating' static becomes a primary (AD 1). If you see [1/0] in show ip route, that's AD 1 / metric 0 = a default-AD static route.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, configure one static route of each variant on the same router: (1) next-hop IP, (2) exit interface only (on a P2P serial), (3) fully specified, (4) floating with AD 210. Run 'show ip route' and compare the entries. Delete each with the corresponding 'no ip route ...' command and watch the RIB shrink. This sweeps every legal syntax in one 10-minute lab.
hack.meta: Syntax questions appear as lab sims. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 walks through each variant. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Memorize the command order — destination BEFORE mask BEFORE next-hop. The exam expects clean typing, not autocomplete guessing.

3.3.b.info:
Every static route installed with the basic ip route command (no trailing AD number) has an administrative distance of 1. This is baked into IOS and cannot be changed globally — the only way to raise it is per-route by appending a number: ip route ... 210.

Why AD 1: AD is Cisco's trust ranking: lower = more trusted. Directly connected interfaces are AD 0 (the router physically sees them, cannot be more trustworthy). Static routes are AD 1 — 'a human explicitly typed this in, so trust it over any dynamic protocol.' This is by design: if you hand-configure a route, Cisco assumes you mean it and want it to win over OSPF, EIGRP, RIP, BGP, everything.

The wins-everything side effect: A static route at AD 1 beats OSPF (AD 110), EIGRP (AD 90), RIP (AD 120), and internal BGP (AD 200) for the same prefix. This is usually what you want for permanent overrides. But if you intended the static to be a BACKUP (floating static), leaving AD at 1 silently turns the dynamic protocol into the backup instead — the opposite of what you wanted. This is the #1 floating-static mistake.

AD table worth memorizing:

  • Connected: 0
  • Static: 1
  • eBGP: 20
  • EIGRP internal: 90
  • OSPF: 110
  • RIP: 120
  • EIGRP external: 170
  • iBGP: 200
  • Floating/unknown: 255

How to verify: show ip route 10.1.1.0 returns the active route with its AD in brackets — [1/0] means AD 1, metric 0. If you see [210/0] on a static route, the AD was appended to the command.

Strategic note: On the exam, when you see two routes for the same prefix and a 'which wins?' question, check prefix length FIRST, then AD. A /24 static at AD 1 still loses to a /32 from any source because longest-prefix-match runs before AD comparison.

hack.memory: Static = AD 1, second only to Connected (AD 0). Static crushes every dynamic protocol unless you manually raise the AD. 'I typed it, I meant it' = AD 1. Rule of thumb for floating statics: pick AD = (primary AD + 100) to leave a big margin. OSPF primary (110) → floating 210.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure OSPF between two routers for 10.1.1.0/24 — observe 'O 10.1.1.0/24 [110/...]' in the table. Now add 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 [different-next-hop]' on one router. Re-check the table: the route is now 'S 10.1.1.0/24 [1/0]' — the OSPF route lost because AD 1 < AD 110. Remove the static, OSPF comes back. This swap cements the AD-1 behavior.
hack.meta: Guaranteed CCNA content. Jeremy's IT Lab Days 11 and 24. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Expect one scenario where a static route accidentally overrides a dynamic route — the answer is 'static has AD 1, dynamic had higher AD, static wins.'

3.3.b.info:
Bidirectional routing is the rule that for end-to-end IP communication to work, BOTH directions of the path must have routing in place. It's not enough for R1 to know how to reach R3's LAN — R3 must also know how to reach R1's LAN, or the reply packets have nowhere to go.

Why it's easy to miss: Network engineers think in terms of the forward path ('how do packets get to the destination?'). But every TCP/ICMP/application session is two-way — the server has to send replies back to the client. If R3 has no route to R1's LAN, the reply packets hit R3's default drop behavior (Destination Unreachable or silent drop, depending on whether a default route exists). From R1's perspective, this looks like 'request timed out' with no clue about where the failure happened.

Classic symptoms: Ping shows 'Request timed out' (not 'Destination host unreachable'). Traceroute from R1 shows the packet reaching R3 successfully but no replies after that hop. Running a capture on R3's LAN interface shows incoming echo requests but no outgoing echo replies because R3 can't figure out where to send them.

The fix: Configure static routes in BOTH directions. Example — R1 has LAN 10.1.0.0/24, R3 has LAN 10.3.0.0/24, linked via 192.168.1.0/24 through R2:

  • On R1: ip route 10.3.0.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2 (forward path)
  • On R3: ip route 10.1.0.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.1 (return path)

Without both, the ping fails. With both, it succeeds.

Extended ping for diagnosis: ping 10.3.0.5 source 10.1.0.1 forces the source address to be from R1's LAN. If the normal ping works but the sourced ping fails, it's almost certainly a missing return route — R3 can reach R1's transit IP but not R1's LAN prefix. This is THE most powerful static-routing troubleshooting command.

Exam trap: Scenarios give you a topology with 4 routers and ask 'why doesn't end-to-end ping work?' The answer is always 'missing return route on Rx.' Train yourself to check both directions every time.

steps: Only one present = request timed out

hack.memory: Routing is a two-way street. Forward route + return route = working ping. Forward only = 'request timed out' with zero error clues. Every time you add a static route, ask: 'what's the return route and does it exist?' Extended ping with 'source' is your detective tool.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, build R1-R2-R3 with LANs on each end. Configure the forward static route only (R1 knows how to reach R3's LAN but R3 has no return). Ping from R1's LAN to R3's LAN and observe 'Request timed out.' Run 'debug ip packet' on R3 to see the request arriving but no matching reply leaving. Add the return static — pings succeed instantly. Repeat the exercise three times until the muscle memory 'both directions always' is automatic.
hack.meta: #1 static-routing exam trap. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 demonstrates this exact scenario. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Almost every static-route troubleshooting sim on the CCNA hinges on bidirectional routing. Develop the reflex: 'new static route? Check the return route too.'

3.3.b.info:
The IPv6 static route syntax is ipv6 route [prefix/prefix-length] [next-hop | exit-interface] [optional-AD]. Structurally identical to IPv4's ip route command with one difference: IPv6 uses CIDR prefix notation directly (e.g., 2001:DB8:1::/64) instead of a separate dotted-decimal mask.

Three variants, same semantics as IPv4:

  • Recursive (next-hop global unicast): ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 2001:DB8:2::1
  • Directly attached (exit interface only): ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 GigabitEthernet0/0
  • Fully specified (both interface and next-hop): ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 GigabitEthernet0/0 2001:DB8:2::1

Link-local exception: If the next-hop is a link-local address (fe80::/10), you MUST specify the exit interface — the router cannot otherwise determine which interface the fe80:: address lives on (link-local addresses are only unique per-interface, not globally). Example: ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/0 fe80::1. This is the most-tested IPv6 static rule on the CCNA.

Enabling IPv6 routing: Before ANY IPv6 static route works, you must globally enable ipv6 unicast-routing. Without it, the router will accept the route command, show it in running-config, but silently drop all transit IPv6 traffic. This is the #1 IPv6 'I configured everything and nothing works' trap.

Verification commands: show ipv6 route (full table), show ipv6 route static (static entries only), show ipv6 route [prefix] (lookup for a specific prefix), show running-config | include ipv6 route (configured routes from the config). Static IPv6 routes appear with code S: S 2001:DB8:1::/64 [1/0] via 2001:DB8:2::1.

Bidirectional rule still applies: Just like IPv4, every IPv6 static route needs a return path or traffic fails. Forgetting ipv6 unicast-routing on the return router is an even sneakier variant — the route exists, the command looks right, but forwarding is off.

hack.memory: Same structure, IPv6 flavor. CIDR inline (no separate mask). Two must-remembers: (1) if next-hop is fe80::, ALWAYS add the exit interface. (2) 'ipv6 unicast-routing' must be globally enabled or nothing forwards. Miss either and your perfect-looking config silently fails.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure an IPv6-only 3-router chain. On each router enable 'ipv6 unicast-routing' and assign global unicast addresses. Configure static IPv6 routes using global unicast next-hops — verify with 'show ipv6 route.' Then on one router replace the next-hop with fe80:: WITHOUT the interface — watch IOS reject or ambiguously accept the command. Fix with the exit interface and confirm the route installs. Do this lab enough times that the link-local+interface rule becomes reflexive.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 31-33 cover IPv6 in depth. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 23-25. Expect 2-3 IPv6 static-route questions on the exam, often hinging on link-local syntax or the unicast-routing prerequisite. Don't treat IPv6 as 'IPv4 with longer addresses' — the gotchas are distinctive.

3.3.c.info:
A host route is a route with a /32 prefix length (subnet mask 255.255.255.255 in IPv4 or /128 in IPv6), matching exactly one IP address. With all 32 bits of the mask set to 1, every single bit of the destination must match — there is no flexibility, no host portion, just one specific IP.

Why /32 matters: /32 is the longest possible prefix in IPv4, which makes it the MOST specific route possible. Under the longest-prefix-match rule, a /32 wins against /24, /16, /8, /0, or any other prefix length. This is true regardless of the route's source — a /32 learned from RIP beats a /24 from Connected.

Two origins:

  • Automatic L routes — The router creates a /32 'local' route for every IP configured on one of its own interfaces. Gi0/0 with 10.1.1.1/24 produces BOTH C 10.1.1.0/24 AND L 10.1.1.1/32.
  • Manual static host routes — An admin configures ip route 10.1.1.100 255.255.255.255 192.168.1.2 to force traffic for exactly 10.1.1.100 through a specific path.

Use cases for manual /32 routes: (1) Policy-based steering — force a specific server's traffic through a particular firewall or path. (2) Blackholing — send a malicious host's traffic to Null0: ip route 203.0.113.99 255.255.255.255 Null0. (3) Overriding a broader route for one exception — the /24 normally goes left, but this one /32 goes right. (4) Fast-switching certain critical services through a preferred path.

IPv6 equivalent: /128 is the IPv6 host route. ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::100/128 2001:DB8:2::1. Same concept, longer addresses. Every IPv6 interface also gets an automatic /128 L route for its own address.

Danger: A misconfigured /32 can silently hijack traffic. Because it always wins LPM, a typo that creates an unintended /32 can route one specific IP to nowhere while every other IP in the same subnet works normally. Always check for unexpected /32 entries when troubleshooting 'one specific host is broken.'

hack.memory: /32 = GPS pin on one house. All 32 bits network, zero host bits, one address total. Longest possible prefix = ALWAYS wins longest-prefix-match. The L entries in 'show ip route' are auto-/32s for your own IPs. Manual /32 = precision scalpel for traffic steering OR blackhole.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: run 'show ip route' and identify every L entry (auto /32s). Configure a manual /32: 'ip route 10.1.1.100 255.255.255.255 192.168.1.2' and verify it appears as 'S 10.1.1.100/32 [1/0] via 192.168.1.2.' Now create a broader static 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 [different next-hop]' and trace a packet to 10.1.1.100 — watch it follow the /32, not the /24. Remove the /32 and it falls back to the /24.
hack.meta: Tested indirectly — the exam presents scenarios where /32 wins LPM and asks 'which path does the packet take?' Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 15-16. Know that /32 > /28 > /24 > /16 > /8 > /0 for LPM purposes.

3.3.c.info:
The L (local) route is a /32 host route automatically generated by IOS for every IP address configured on the router's own interfaces. It exists alongside the connected (C) route for the interface's subnet and serves a specific internal purpose: ensuring traffic destined TO the router (not THROUGH the router) is processed locally.

What L routes look like: Configure interface Gi0/0, ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0, no shutdown. Run show ip route and you'll see:

C    10.1.1.0/24 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/0
L    10.1.1.1/32 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/0

The C entry (Connected, AD 0) covers the whole subnet — forwarded traffic to anything in 10.1.1.0/24 uses Gi0/0. The L entry (Local, /32) is specifically for 10.1.1.1 — the router's own IP — and tells IOS 'this packet is for me, send it to the CPU for local processing, do not forward it.'

Why it matters: Without the L route, traffic destined for the router's own IP would match the C route (10.1.1.0/24), and the router might try to forward the packet back out Gi0/0 toward 10.1.1.1 — itself — creating a loop. The L route short-circuits this by saying 'this exact /32 is local, not forwarded.'

When L routes appear/disappear: An L route exists only while the interface is up/up with an IP configured. Shut the interface, the L and C entries both vanish instantly. Bring it back up, they return. This tight coupling is how you verify interface health via routing-table inspection: 'no L for 10.1.1.1? Gi0/0 must be down.'

Introduced in IOS 15: L routes are a relatively modern feature — pre-15 IOS only showed C entries and handled local traffic implicitly. Any modern Cisco exam references IOS 15+ behavior, so expect L entries in all sample outputs.

IPv6 equivalent: Every IPv6 address on an interface also produces a /128 local route with the L code. Link-local addresses (fe80::/10) also generate L entries — that's why every IPv6 interface has at least one L route even without a global unicast address configured.

children.name: L 10.1.1.1/32 (local host, for traffic destined TO the router)

hack.memory: L = Local = the router's own IP as a /32. Automatic, can't configure it manually. Every interface IP creates a C (subnet) + L (host) pair. The L route is how the router says 'this packet is for me, not something I forward.' No L = interface down.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: on a fresh router with no interfaces configured, 'show ip route' is empty. Configure 'interface Gi0/0' → 'ip address 10.1.1.1 255.255.255.0' → 'no shutdown.' Re-run the command. You'll see BOTH C 10.1.1.0/24 AND L 10.1.1.1/32. Shut Gi0/0 — both disappear. Bring it back up — both return. The 1:1 relationship between interface state and C/L pair becomes obvious after one cycle.
hack.meta: Recognition-level content on the exam. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 and Day 15 both mention L routes. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. You won't be asked to 'configure an L route' (you can't), but you need to recognize it in 'show ip route' output and know it means the interface is up with that IP.

3.3.c.info:
While automatic L routes cover a router's own IPs, manual host routes (admin-configured /32 static routes) are a deliberate tool for shaping traffic at a per-IP level. The command is identical to a network static route, but with a /32 mask: ip route [host-IP] 255.255.255.255 [next-hop | interface].

Use case 1 — Blackholing malicious traffic: Send all traffic to a known bad IP into the Null0 interface, which silently discards packets. ip route 203.0.113.99 255.255.255.255 Null0. This is the simplest form of IP blocking — no ACL, no firewall rule, just a /32 to nowhere. Used for Remote Triggered Black Hole (RTBH) DDoS mitigation at ISP scale.

Use case 2 — Policy-based steering for one host: A CEO's laptop should always route through a specific VPN path while everyone else on the same subnet uses the default path. ip route 10.1.1.99 255.255.255.255 192.168.99.1 forces just that one IP through the VPN next-hop. Because /32 always wins LPM, it overrides the broader /24 route for that specific host.

Use case 3 — Inter-VRF or management-traffic steering: Push traffic to a specific management server (say, a jump host at 10.99.99.10) through a dedicated out-of-band interface, leaving all other traffic to use the primary path. Common on core routers with multiple management networks.

Use case 4 — Overriding a troublesome broader route temporarily: A /16 aggregate is causing problems for one IP, and you need a surgical fix without tearing down the whole aggregate. Configure a /32 pointing at the correct path until the underlying issue is resolved.

Caution — operational hazard: Because /32s win LPM against everything, they're easy to forget and hard to spot in a big table. Always document manual /32 routes in change management. When troubleshooting 'one specific host behaves weird,' your first check should be show ip route [that-host] to see if a forgotten /32 is routing it unexpectedly.

IPv6 equivalent: ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::99/128 2001:DB8:2::1. Same strategy, same cautions.

right.label: Host static (/32)
items: Used for surgical steering or blackhole

hack.memory: Manual /32 = precision tool. Use it to steer one IP through a specific next-hop, blackhole a bad IP via Null0, or carve one exception out of a broader route. Easy to forget, always wins LPM — comment your configs so future-you doesn't hunt for an invisible override.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure a /24 static route on R1 pointing 10.1.1.0/24 through one path. Add a /32: 'ip route 10.1.1.99 255.255.255.255 [different next-hop].' Ping 10.1.1.5 (uses /24 path) and 10.1.1.99 (uses /32 path) from R1 and trace both. For a blackhole demo: 'ip route 10.1.1.99 255.255.255.255 Null0' — pings to 10.1.1.99 now die silently while 10.1.1.5 still works. Watch 'show ip route' for both entries to confirm both exist.
hack.meta: Intermediate CCNA content — not as heavily tested as default routes or floating statics but can appear. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Recognize /32 scenarios in diagrams: if one host is 'special,' a manual /32 is likely involved.

3.3.d.info:
A floating static route is a static route that sits dormant in the configuration with a higher-than-normal administrative distance, ready to activate only when a preferred (lower-AD) route disappears. It 'floats' ABOVE the routing table — present in running-config but not in the RIB — until the primary route goes away.

The mechanic: The router's RIB selects ONE best route per destination prefix using (1) longest prefix match, (2) lowest administrative distance, (3) lowest metric. A floating static is crafted so its AD is HIGHER than the primary source. Example: primary is OSPF (AD 110), floating static AD is 210. OSPF wins comparison step 2 (110 < 210), so OSPF installs into the RIB. The floating static remains configured but hidden from show ip route. The instant OSPF loses the route (neighbor dead, link down), the floating static becomes the only candidate and jumps into the table.

Failover flow:

  • Normal operation: OSPF route active, AD 110, in RIB. Floating static inactive, AD 210, NOT in RIB.
  • Primary fails: OSPF route removed from RIB. Floating static now has no competitor for that prefix, gets installed.
  • Primary recovers: OSPF route reappears with AD 110 < 210, reclaims the RIB slot. Floating static goes dormant again.

Why use it: It's the simplest possible backup strategy. No extra routing protocol, no neighbor relationships, no additional CPU. Perfect for branch-office scenarios: primary link uses OSPF or the main default route, secondary link (backup MPLS, LTE, secondary ISP) uses a floating static. Zero ongoing overhead, automatic failover.

Common mistakes: (1) Forgetting to append the AD — static defaults to AD 1, which is LOWER than any dynamic protocol, so the 'backup' silently becomes the primary. (2) Setting the AD too low (e.g., 100 when primary is OSPF 110 — floating wins). (3) Pointing at a next-hop that's unreachable even when 'primary' is up — the floating static can't install because the next-hop isn't usable. (4) Forgetting bidirectional routing — the backup path also needs a return route, which may be missing because the primary return path was handling everything before.

Works on IPv4 and IPv6: ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.2.2 210 or ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 2001:DB8:BACKUP::1 210. Same pattern, different command prefix.

states: Primary recovers — OSPF wins again, floating goes dormant

hack.memory: Floating static = basement generator. Higher AD keeps it out of the RIB. When primary fails (power cut), it activates automatically. When primary recovers, it goes back to standby. The magic is the AD — set it ABOVE the primary's AD with a safety margin (e.g., primary 110 → floating 210).
hack.practice: Packet Tracer lab: (1) Configure OSPF between R1 and R2 for 10.1.1.0/24. Verify 'O 10.1.1.0/24 [110/...]' in R1's RIB. (2) On R1 add 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 [backup-next-hop] 210.' (3) 'show ip route' — only the OSPF route shows, floating hides. (4) Shut the OSPF-facing interface. (5) 'show ip route' — floating static now shows as 'S 10.1.1.0/24 [210/0].' (6) Bring OSPF back up — OSPF reclaims the slot. Repeat until failover muscle memory is automatic.
hack.meta: Heavy CCNA exam topic. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 covers floating statics directly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Expect a configuration sim question: 'configure a backup path that activates only when the primary fails.' If you forget to append the AD, the sim marks it wrong.

3.3.d.info:
Cisco IOS provides a single method to override the default AD on a static route: append the AD value to the end of the ip route (or ipv6 route) command. The syntax is ip route [network] [mask] [next-hop|interface] [AD] where the trailing AD is any integer from 1 to 255.

Examples:

  • ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2 — default AD 1 (no override)
  • ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2 5 — slightly elevated AD 5 (rarely used)
  • ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.2.2 210 — floating static, dormant while OSPF (110) is up
  • ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.2.2 255 — AD 255 = 'never use this route' (special meaning: never installed)

Valid AD range 1-255: AD 0 is reserved for directly connected routes and cannot be set manually. AD 255 is a special 'infinite' marker — a route with AD 255 is NEVER installed in the RIB (used as a way to effectively disable a route without removing it from config).

Default AD reminder: Static routes without the trailing AD get AD 1 — this is not a 'neutral' value. It's actually very low, beating every dynamic protocol. Engineers who assume 'default AD is like a middle number' get surprised when their intended backup overrides their primary. The default is aggressive; you must explicitly raise AD for a backup role.

Picking the right AD value: Convention is to set floating AD well above the primary protocol's AD for a safety buffer. Common choices:

  • Backup for OSPF (AD 110): floating AD 210
  • Backup for EIGRP (AD 90): floating AD 190
  • Backup for another static (AD 1): floating AD 100 or 200
  • Generic 'very low priority': AD 250

IPv6 equivalent: ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 2001:DB8:2::1 210. Append the AD just like IPv4.

Verifying the AD: show ip route 10.1.1.0 returns the active route with its AD in brackets: [210/0]. show running-config | include ip route shows the configured command (including the AD number). Use both checks — running-config shows intent, show ip route shows reality.

right.label: Floating static (AD 210)
items: Not in table while primary active

hack.memory: The AD goes at the END of the ip route command — ALWAYS. No AD = AD 1 (aggressive primary). With AD = custom value (backup/override role). Pick AD at least 10 ABOVE the protocol you're backing up, or go with the standard 'primary AD + 100' formula.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2' and verify [1/0] in 'show ip route.' Delete it. Configure 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2 210' and verify [210/0]. Now add OSPF advertising the same prefix with AD 110. The AD-210 static disappears from the table (AD 110 wins). Shut the OSPF interface — the static reappears. Toggle between values (50, 100, 150, 250) and confirm each installs correctly with the given AD.
hack.meta: Guaranteed exam content on floating static configuration. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. The biggest trap is forgetting the trailing AD in lab sims — graders mark it wrong. Double-check the command ends with the AD number before hitting enter.

3.3.d.info:
For a floating static to actually 'float' (stay out of the RIB until needed), its AD must be HIGHER than the primary route's AD. If the floating AD is lower or equal, the router will either install the floating static instead of the primary, or behave unpredictably on tie-break — defeating the backup purpose entirely.

The arithmetic: Cisco's route-selection step 2 is 'lowest AD wins.' Primary OSPF has AD 110. For a floating static to LOSE that comparison, its AD must be greater than 110. Any value 111-255 works. Convention is to use 200, 210, or 250 for a clear margin.

Why margin matters: Picking AD 111 technically works but leaves zero room for configuration mistakes or protocol-variant quirks. Picking AD 210 makes the intent obvious at a glance and survives future changes (e.g., if someone later tweaks the primary's AD or adds a different dynamic protocol).

Common AD pairings (primary → backup):

  • OSPF (110) → floating static 210
  • EIGRP internal (90) → floating static 190 or 210
  • RIP (120) → floating static 220
  • eBGP (20) → floating static 120 or 200
  • iBGP (200) → floating static 250
  • Primary static (AD 1) → floating static 100 or 200 (for static-backs-up-static scenarios)

Static-to-static backup gotcha: If you want one static to back up another static, the primary is AD 1 and the backup can be literally anything ≥ 2. Writing ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 [primary] 5 and ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 [backup] 100 is valid — the AD 5 route is the primary, the AD 100 route floats. But using AD 1 explicitly on the primary (which is the default) is cleaner.

Fail scenario — AD too low: Configure ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 [backup] 50 when the primary is OSPF at AD 110. The 'floating' static actually has LOWER AD than OSPF, so it wins — the static becomes primary, OSPF becomes effective backup. The admin thinks 'OSPF is primary' but the RIB shows 'S [50/0] via [backup].' This is a subtle, silent failure mode.

How to verify the pairing: After configuring, run show ip route [prefix] and confirm the active route is the INTENDED primary (OSPF/EIGRP/whatever). If you see your static in the table during normal operation, your AD is too low.

hack.memory: Rule: floating AD > primary AD. Simple, absolute. Convention: primary AD + 100 for a clear margin (OSPF 110 → 210). If primary is static (AD 1), pick any AD ≥ 2 for the backup. Verify by running 'show ip route [prefix]' during normal ops — you should see the PRIMARY, not your floating.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, set up OSPF between R1-R2 for 10.1.1.0/24. On R1 configure three floating static variants in sequence: (a) AD 50 — observe that the static takes over and OSPF is displaced (wrong!). (b) AD 110 — same AD as OSPF, unpredictable behavior. (c) AD 210 — correct, static hides, OSPF primary. Remove each between tests. This failure/success sweep makes 'AD must exceed primary' a reflex instead of a rule to remember.
hack.meta: Exam scenario favorite: 'given these ADs, which route is active?' You must know primary ADs cold AND the >primary rule for floating statics. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. The trap is always a too-low floating AD silently becoming primary.

3.3.d.info:
The most common error when building a floating static route is simply forgetting to append the AD value. Without the trailing number, the command ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 [backup-next-hop] creates a regular static with AD 1 — which immediately overrides any dynamic protocol advertising the same prefix. The intended 'backup' silently becomes the primary, and the actual primary (OSPF, EIGRP, whatever) is never used.

Why this fails silently: The command succeeds, the route installs, the config looks fine. show ip route shows S 10.1.1.0/24 [1/0] via [backup-next-hop] — exactly what you typed, except via the backup path. Pings work because the static route is reachable. Everything LOOKS right unless you specifically check 'which route is active for this prefix?' and notice the AD is 1 instead of the intended 210.

Why it's so common: The trailing AD is OPTIONAL syntactically. IOS doesn't warn you when you omit it. Students memorize 'ip route [network] [mask] [next-hop]' from earlier lessons and don't internalize the extra parameter until they configure floating statics for the first time. Under exam-timer pressure, the AD gets forgotten.

How to catch it:

  • After configuring, immediately run show ip route [prefix]. If you see your static in the table during normal operations (primary is up), the AD is too low.
  • Show running-config: show running-config | include ip route shows the actual command text. If it ends with the next-hop IP and nothing after, the AD defaulted to 1.
  • Run 'show ip route static': if your floating static appears while the primary is supposed to be up, it's not floating — it's active.

Secondary common mistakes:

  1. AD too low — setting AD 50 when primary is OSPF (110). Same outcome as forgetting AD entirely: the 'floating' static wins.
  2. Wrong next-hop on backup — typing the primary's next-hop instead of the backup path, so both 'routes' go the same direction and failover is useless.
  3. Missing return route on backup path — the primary path worked both ways due to symmetric dynamic routing, but the backup path is asymmetric and needs its own return static.
  4. Unreachable backup next-hop — the backup IP is in a subnet that's itself unreachable. Floating static can't install because the next-hop can't be resolved.

Rule of thumb: After every floating static config, force-test the failover: shut the primary interface, confirm the floating activates. Un-shut, confirm the primary reclaims. If either step fails, your config has one of these bugs.

left.label: WRONG (AD default = 1)
items: Static is active, OSPF is unused

hack.memory: Forgotten AD = AD 1 = instant primary override. The TRAILING NUMBER is the floating static — miss it and your backup becomes your primary. ALWAYS verify with 'show ip route [prefix]' right after configuring. If the static shows up during normal ops, your AD is wrong (too low or missing).
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: deliberately configure a floating static WITHOUT the AD number. Confirm it wins against OSPF in 'show ip route.' Delete and re-add WITH the AD (e.g., 210). Confirm OSPF reclaims the slot. This 'wrong vs right' A/B drill cements why the trailing AD is non-optional for backup routes. Build a personal checklist: (1) configure floating, (2) verify primary still active, (3) shut primary, (4) verify floating activates, (5) un-shut primary, (6) verify failback.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 explicitly warns about this. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Expect exam scenarios where a 'backup' static is inadvertently active — answer is 'AD was not set.' Also expect sims where you have to configure a floating correctly — miss the AD and you lose the points.

3.3.d.info:
Failover behavior for a floating static route is automatic and tied directly to the RIB's route-selection logic. When the primary route is withdrawn (interface down, neighbor dead, protocol loss), the RIB re-evaluates candidates for that prefix and installs the next-best — which is the floating static.

Primary fails → floating activates:

  1. Primary source (e.g., OSPF) loses the route. Trigger can be: neighbor goes DOWN, interface shuts, protocol loses reachability, authentication fails, route gets filtered out.
  2. OSPF removes O 10.1.1.0/24 [110/x] from the RIB.
  3. RIB scans remaining candidates for 10.1.1.0/24. The floating static (AD 210) is the only option left.
  4. RIB installs S 10.1.1.0/24 [210/0] via [backup-next-hop].
  5. Traffic to 10.1.1.0/24 now flows out the backup path.

Primary recovers → floating goes dormant:

  1. Primary source (OSPF) re-advertises the route — neighbor comes back up, interface recovers, etc.
  2. RIB compares candidates: OSPF AD 110 vs floating static AD 210. Lower AD wins.
  3. OSPF route installs, floating static is pulled from the RIB back into standby.
  4. Traffic returns to the primary path.

Failover timing: How fast this happens depends on how fast the primary route is declared dead.

  • Interface down: Near-instant. IOS detects carrier loss and withdraws the route within milliseconds.
  • OSPF neighbor dead: Dead interval (default 40s on broadcast/P2P). Until the dead timer fires, OSPF still considers the route valid.
  • EIGRP neighbor lost: Hold time (default 15s on Ethernet). DUAL runs and feasible successors take over.
  • BGP session down: Hold timer (default 180s). BFD can cut this dramatically.

To accelerate failover: Use BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) or aggressive hello/dead timers. These are beyond CCNA scope but worth knowing exist — real networks often pair floating statics with sub-second link detection.

Dampening on flap: If the primary link flaps rapidly (up, down, up, down), the floating static flaps with it. Traffic stability suffers. Production networks add dampening or IP SLA tracking to avoid oscillation — again, beyond CCNA but worth naming.

Verification during failover: Watch show ip route 10.1.1.0 in real time while you toggle the primary interface. Before: O route with AD 110. After shut: S route with AD 210. After no-shut (and OSPF reconverges): back to O.

states: RIB reclaims OSPF (floating back to dormant)

hack.memory: Failover = RIB re-selects when primary is withdrawn. Floating static installs INSTANTLY once the primary disappears — but 'disappears' depends on detection speed: interface down = ms, OSPF dead = 40s, BGP = 180s. On exam scenarios, always ask 'what triggers the primary withdrawal?' to estimate failover time.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: OSPF between R1-R2 for 10.1.1.0/24 + floating static on R1 (AD 210). Split the screen: one window runs 'show ip route 10.1.1.0' in a loop (or re-run every 5s). Other window: shut the OSPF-facing interface. Watch the RIB entry change from 'O 10.1.1.0/24 [110/...]' to 'S 10.1.1.0/24 [210/0]' within a few seconds (dead timer). Un-shut, watch it flip back. Repeat with an Ethernet flap vs a serial shutdown to observe timing differences.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 and Day 24 (OSPF) combined cover failover timing. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 15 and 19-21. Exam scenarios often ask 'which route is in the RIB RIGHT NOW given these events?' — you have to mentally run the state machine. Floating static + OSPF/EIGRP is the classic pairing.

3.3.e.info:
The IPv6 static route syntax is ipv6 route [prefix/length] [next-hop | exit-interface | both] [optional-AD]. The structure exactly mirrors ip route for IPv4, with one syntactic change: the prefix and length are combined into CIDR notation inline (e.g., 2001:DB8:1::/64) instead of using a separate dotted-decimal mask.

Supported variants (identical semantics to IPv4):

  • Recursive (next-hop only): ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 2001:DB8:2::1. Router recurses to find the exit interface for 2001:DB8:2::1.
  • Directly attached (exit interface only): ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 GigabitEthernet0/0. Skips recursion, appropriate only on point-to-point links.
  • Fully specified: ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 GigabitEthernet0/0 2001:DB8:2::1. Best practice on Ethernet — no recursion AND no ARP-per-destination issue.
  • With AD override (floating): ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 2001:DB8:BACKUP::1 210.

Default route form: ipv6 route ::/0 [next-hop | interface]. The ::/0 prefix matches every IPv6 destination and serves as the gateway of last resort.

Link-local next-hop rule (critical): When the next-hop is a link-local address (anything in fe80::/10), you MUST include the exit interface. Example: ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/0 fe80::1. Without the interface, IOS either rejects the command or installs an ambiguous route, because link-local addresses repeat across interfaces and the router can't resolve which one the fe80::1 lives on.

Global prerequisite: ipv6 unicast-routing must be enabled globally. Without it, IPv6 static routes can be configured but the router will NOT forward IPv6 packets — local processing only. This is the #1 'my config is perfect and nothing works' trap in IPv6.

Removal: no ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 2001:DB8:2::1. Match the exact command used to configure.

Where it appears: Active routes show up in show ipv6 route with code S: S 2001:DB8:1::/64 [1/0] via 2001:DB8:2::1. show ipv6 route static filters to static-only. show running-config | section ipv6 (or | include ipv6 route) shows configured commands.

hack.memory: Same shape as IPv4, different dialect. Prefix/length inline. Next-hop CAN be fe80:: but then you MUST add the exit interface. AD trails (for floating). And always-always-always enable 'ipv6 unicast-routing' globally or nothing forwards.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, build an IPv6-only lab: 2 routers connected via 2001:DB8:FF::/64 transit, each with a LAN (2001:DB8:1::/64, 2001:DB8:2::/64). Enable 'ipv6 unicast-routing' globally. Configure 'ipv6 address' on interfaces. Add static routes using global unicast next-hops — verify with 'show ipv6 route.' Then try link-local next-hops (fe80::) with and without exit interfaces — observe the behavior. Finish with a default: 'ipv6 route ::/0 Gi0/0 fe80::2.'
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Days 31-33 cover IPv6 static routing. Wendell Odom OCG Chapters 23-25. Exam will absolutely test the syntax — especially the link-local + interface combo. Don't rely on IPv4 muscle memory; IPv6 has distinctive rules that must be practiced.

3.3.e.info:
ipv6 unicast-routing is the global command that enables IPv6 packet forwarding on a Cisco router. Without it, the router processes IPv6 traffic addressed to itself (ping to its own interfaces works) but will NOT forward IPv6 packets between interfaces — every transit IPv6 packet is silently dropped.

Why it's a separate switch: Historically, IPv6 was disabled by default on Cisco routers to avoid accidental unintended forwarding. Even today, you have to explicitly opt in to 'I want this router to route IPv6.' IPv4 routing is usually on by default on routers (ip routing), but IPv6 always requires the explicit command.

Configuration: One line, at global config level: ipv6 unicast-routing. No parameters, no options. Either it's on or it's not.

How to verify:

  • show running-config | include ipv6 unicast-routing — confirms the command is in the config.
  • show ipv6 interface brief — doesn't directly show forwarding state, but interfaces will have IPv6 addresses even without the command.
  • show ipv6 traffic — shows IPv6 forwarding counters; if 'unicast-routing' is off, the 'forwarded' counter stays at 0 even with traffic.

Common symptoms of missing unicast-routing:

  • All IPv6 static routes are configured correctly.
  • 'show ipv6 route' shows the static routes as installed.
  • But end-to-end pings across the router fail.
  • Debug 'ipv6 packet' shows packets arriving but no 'forwarding' events.

L3 switch analogue: On a Layer 3 switch, IPv4 routing requires ip routing in global config (it's off by default on switches). IPv6 routing on the same switch requires ipv6 unicast-routing. Forgetting either leaves the switch acting like a Layer 2 device for that protocol family.

Related command: ipv6 cef (also enabled by default on modern IOS) accelerates IPv6 forwarding via CEF, similar to IPv4's CEF. Without CEF, IPv6 uses slower process switching.

Troubleshooting checklist when IPv6 pings fail: (1) Is 'ipv6 unicast-routing' in the config? (2) Are the interfaces up/up with IPv6 addresses? (3) Is the static route installed in 'show ipv6 route'? (4) Is there a return route on the other end? All four must be yes.

params.title: ipv6 unicast-routing
layers: With: router forwards IPv6 between interfaces
Applies globally — one command per router
Analogous to ip routing (IPv4) on L3 switches

hack.memory: ONE global command unlocks IPv6 routing. Forget it and your 'perfect' IPv6 config silently drops every transit packet. First thing you type on any IPv6 router, every time: 'ipv6 unicast-routing' in global config. The router doesn't warn you it's missing.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure IPv6 addresses on two router interfaces WITHOUT enabling 'ipv6 unicast-routing.' Configure static routes between them. Try a ping — it fails. Run 'show ipv6 route' — the routes exist. Enable 'ipv6 unicast-routing' globally. Ping again — works. This A/B test cements the command's role. Add it to your IPv6 configuration checklist as line 1.
hack.meta: High-value exam fact. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 31 introduces it explicitly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. The exam asks it two ways: 'what command enables IPv6 routing?' (answer: ipv6 unicast-routing) and 'why is this IPv6 config not forwarding?' (answer: ipv6 unicast-routing is missing). Expect at least one question.

3.3.e.info:
When an IPv6 static route's next-hop is a link-local address (any address in fe80::/10), the command MUST also specify the exit interface. This is the most heavily tested IPv6 static-routing rule on the CCNA.

Correct syntax: ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/0 fe80::1. Both the interface AND the fe80:: address are present. Interface comes BEFORE the next-hop in the command.

Incorrect syntax (will fail or behave ambiguously): ipv6 route ::/0 fe80::1. This has a link-local next-hop with no interface — IOS either rejects it outright or installs an unusable route. Depends on IOS version, but the result is the same: traffic doesn't flow.

Where this appears in the real world: Link-local addresses are used extensively in IPv6 — every IPv6-enabled interface automatically gets a fe80:: address. Many routing protocols (OSPF v3, EIGRP for IPv6) use link-local as next-hops for neighbors on the same link. When you manually configure a static that mirrors a dynamic adjacency — say, a backup static pointing at the same link-local that OSPFv3 uses — you naturally end up using fe80:: syntax.

Global unicast next-hop alternative: You can avoid the issue entirely by using the next-hop router's global unicast address instead: ipv6 route ::/0 2001:DB8:FEED::1. No exit interface needed because global unicast addresses are globally unique. This is often the cleaner approach for static default routes.

Why global unicast doesn't need the interface: A global unicast address (2001::/3, etc.) is unique across the entire IPv6 internet. When the router sees 2001:DB8:FEED::1 as a next-hop, it can perform a recursive lookup in its own IPv6 RIB to find which interface leads to that address. No ambiguity. Link-local addresses lack this uniqueness because they repeat across interfaces.

hack.memory: fe80:: next-hop REQUIRES exit interface. Command order: interface FIRST, then fe80::. Or skip the hassle entirely — use a global unicast next-hop (2001::) and no interface is needed. This is the #1 IPv6 static-routing gotcha on the CCNA.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure R1 with an IPv6 interface and try 'ipv6 route ::/0 fe80::2' (no interface) — observe the error or broken route. Fix with 'ipv6 route ::/0 Gi0/0 fe80::2' — installs cleanly. Replace with global unicast: 'ipv6 route ::/0 2001:DB8:FF::2' (no interface) — also installs cleanly. Three variants, three outcomes. Make this A/B/C drill automatic so the rule sticks without thinking.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 31-33 drills this explicitly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Nearly every CCNA IPv6 static-route question has a 'wrong link-local syntax' distractor answer. Know the rule cold — interface + link-local together, always, or use global unicast alone.

3.3.e.info:
The reason link-local next-hops require an exit interface comes down to IPv6 address scoping. Link-local addresses (fe80::/10) are only guaranteed unique on a SINGLE link — not globally, not network-wide, not even across interfaces on the same router. The same fe80::1 can legally exist on every interface a router has.

The ambiguity: Suppose R1 has three interfaces: Gi0/0, Gi0/1, and Gi0/2. Each neighbor router on each interface could have configured fe80::1 as its own address (they're all on different links, so no conflict). When you tell R1 ipv6 route ::/0 fe80::1, which of the three neighbors do you mean? IOS has no way to know — link-local addresses lack the global uniqueness that global unicast addresses have.

Contrast with global unicast: If a neighbor's address is 2001:DB8:FEED::1, that address is (by design) unique across the entire IPv6 internet. When you write ipv6 route ::/0 2001:DB8:FEED::1, the router performs a recursive lookup in its own RIB and finds 2001:DB8:FEED::/64 as a connected route via one specific interface. There's no ambiguity — global unicast addresses pin down to exactly one location.

IPv6 zone IDs (in other contexts): On operating systems like Linux and Windows, link-local addresses are referenced with a 'zone ID' suffix: fe80::1%eth0. The %eth0 is the scope indicator — it tells the OS which interface to associate the address with. Cisco IOS uses the exit interface in the route command to accomplish the same thing.

Why IPv6 uses link-local so much: Link-local addresses are automatically configured on every IPv6 interface without any operator action. They're always present, even on unconfigured interfaces. This makes them ideal for things like Neighbor Discovery (the IPv6 equivalent of ARP), router advertisements, and routing protocol peer relationships — all of which happen on a per-link basis and don't need global uniqueness.

Common scenario: A routing protocol like OSPFv3 or EIGRP for IPv6 uses link-local next-hops for all its routes. When you see show ipv6 route output like O 2001:DB8:1::/64 [110/20] via FE80::1, GigabitEthernet0/0, note that every OSPFv3 route has BOTH a link-local next-hop AND an exit interface — this is why. The routing protocol installs them paired together, because a fe80:: without an interface is meaningless.

children.name: Gi0/2 → yet another neighbor also fe80::1

hack.memory: Link-local = per-link uniqueness only. Same fe80::1 can exist on every link a router connects to. Without the exit interface, 'ipv6 route ::/0 fe80::1' is as ambiguous as 'drive to 123 Main St' — which city? The interface is the city. Global unicast = unique worldwide, no disambiguation needed.
hack.practice: Conceptual practice: on a router with 3 interfaces, imagine each neighbor uses fe80::1. Draw the topology. Write 'ipv6 route ::/0 fe80::1' on a sticky note and ask: which neighbor does this point to? It can't be answered. Now write 'ipv6 route ::/0 Gi0/1 fe80::1' — unambiguous. This thought exercise converts the rule from memorization to understanding.
hack.meta: Understanding-level content. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 32 discusses link-local scope. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. Exam rarely asks 'WHY does link-local need interface?' but knowing the reason helps you recognize scenarios and spot wrong syntax in distractor answers. It also helps you understand OSPFv3/EIGRPv6 outputs with link-local next-hops.

3.3.e.info:
The IPv6 default route is configured as ipv6 route ::/0 [next-hop | interface]. The ::/0 prefix is IPv6's catch-all — equivalent to 0.0.0.0/0 in IPv4. It matches every IPv6 destination and is the least specific route possible, so it only wins when no more specific route exists.

Typical configurations:

  • ipv6 route ::/0 2001:DB8:FEED::1 — recursive via global unicast. Clean, no interface needed.
  • ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/0 fe80::1 — fully specified with link-local. Required syntax when using fe80::.
  • ipv6 route ::/0 GigabitEthernet0/0 — exit interface only. Works on point-to-point but causes Neighbor Discovery flooding on Ethernet, same issue as IPv4.

Meaning of ::/0: The double-colon (::) is shorthand for the all-zeros IPv6 address (0:0:0:0:0:0:0:0). The /0 prefix length means 'zero bits need to match' — so every IPv6 address is a match. Just like IPv4's 0.0.0.0/0, this is mathematically the least specific prefix possible.

Branch office pattern: Just like IPv4, IPv6 branch routers typically have a single default route toward the ISP or HQ. Example: ipv6 route ::/0 2001:DB8:ISP::1. All unknown destinations (read: the entire IPv6 internet) flow through that one next-hop.

Multi-exit scenario: If a site has two WAN links, you can combine defaults with floating-static logic: ipv6 route ::/0 2001:DB8:PRIMARY::1 (default AD 1) + ipv6 route ::/0 2001:DB8:BACKUP::1 210 (floating AD 210). The primary default handles everything until its next-hop becomes unreachable, then the floating takes over.

Prerequisite reminder: ipv6 unicast-routing must be enabled globally or no forwarding happens — even for a perfectly configured default route.

Verification: show ipv6 route ::/0 returns the active default route. show ipv6 route | include ::/0 is a quick check. The route appears as S ::/0 [1/0] via 2001:DB8:FEED::1. Unlike IPv4, IPv6 does NOT print a 'gateway of last resort' line at the top of the table — just look for the S ::/0 entry.

Common mistakes: (1) Typing ::0/0 or other variants instead of ::/0. (2) Using a link-local next-hop without an exit interface. (3) Forgetting ipv6 unicast-routing. (4) Configuring a /128 host route by accident (ipv6 route ::/128 ...) which matches only the all-zeros address and does nothing useful.

hack.memory: ::/0 = IPv6 answer to 0.0.0.0/0. Literal syntax: 'ipv6 route ::/0 [next-hop].' Use global unicast next-hops when possible (no interface needed). Use link-local + exit interface when you must. And ALWAYS have 'ipv6 unicast-routing' enabled or the default does nothing.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure R1 → R2 → simulated IPv6 internet. On R1 enable 'ipv6 unicast-routing' and configure IPv6 on the transit interface. Add 'ipv6 route ::/0 [R2 global unicast]'. Verify 'show ipv6 route' shows the S ::/0 entry. Ping a remote IPv6 address through the default. Remove the default — pings fail. Replace with link-local next-hop + interface — pings work again. This sweeps all three default-route variants.
hack.meta: Guaranteed CCNA content. Jeremy's IT Lab Days 31-33. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 23. The exam tests both the exact syntax (::/0, not 0::/0 or ::0/0) AND the link-local rule. Combined with 'ipv6 unicast-routing,' expect 2-3 IPv6 default-route questions per exam.

3.3.f.info:
A recursive static route specifies the next-hop as an IP address only: ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2. When the router forwards a packet matching this route, it must perform an additional lookup — it searches the routing table AGAIN to figure out which interface leads to 192.168.1.2. This 'route-to-the-next-hop' search is why it's called recursive.

How the lookup works:

  1. Packet arrives for 10.1.1.5.
  2. Router finds S 10.1.1.0/24 [1/0] via 192.168.1.2 — need to reach 192.168.1.2.
  3. Router performs second lookup for 192.168.1.2 — finds C 192.168.1.0/24 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/1.
  4. Router knows: exit Gi0/1, ARP for 192.168.1.2 (or use cached entry), build Ethernet frame, send.

Why this is preferred on Ethernet: On a multi-access medium like Ethernet, many destinations can share the same next-hop. Recursive routes let the router build ONE ARP entry for 192.168.1.2 and reuse it for every destination reachable via that next-hop. Contrast with exit-interface-only routes, which make the router treat every packet as if the destination is directly connected — causing an ARP for every unique destination IP (disaster on Ethernet with lots of traffic).

Trade-off: The extra lookup step costs a tiny bit of CPU per packet. On modern routers with CEF (Cisco Express Forwarding), this cost is negligible — CEF builds a pre-resolved forwarding table that collapses the recursion into a single hardware lookup. Without CEF, recursion is slightly slower than direct exit-interface routing, but still preferred on Ethernet because of the ARP issue.

When recursion fails: If the next-hop IP isn't reachable (no route in the table for it, or its connected interface is down), the recursive route can't resolve, and it's pulled from the RIB. This is actually a feature — it means recursive statics self-remove when their next-hop becomes unreachable, providing a basic form of automatic withdrawal.

Cisco recommendation: Use recursive (next-hop IP only) or fully specified (both interface and next-hop) on Ethernet. NEVER use exit-interface-only on Ethernet for production traffic — the ARP-per-destination behavior will tank performance.

hack.memory: Recursive = 'deliver to this IP' without saying which door. Router searches the RIB twice: once for the destination, once to figure out where the next-hop lives. One ARP per next-hop, regardless of how many destinations use it. Preferred on Ethernet because of ARP efficiency.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2' on R1 (Ethernet link to R2). Ping a host in 10.1.1.0/24 and check 'show arp' on R1 — ONE entry for 192.168.1.2, not one per destination. Now send traffic to multiple destinations in 10.1.1.0/24 — the ARP cache stays at one entry for the next-hop. This efficiency is the whole point.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 explains recursive vs direct. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Exam asks 'which static route type is preferred on Ethernet?' — answer is next-hop IP (recursive) or fully specified. The next-hop-only approach is the most commonly configured style in the real world.

3.3.f.info:
A directly attached (exit-interface-only) static route specifies only the outgoing interface: ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 GigabitEthernet0/0. No next-hop IP. The router treats the destination network as if it were directly connected to that interface.

How forwarding works: Packet arrives for 10.1.1.5, matches the static route, router sends out Gi0/0. But there's a problem: without a next-hop IP, the router doesn't know the destination MAC for the Ethernet frame. On a point-to-point link this is fine — there's only one other device on the line, and most P2P encapsulations (PPP, HDLC) don't need L2 addresses. But on Ethernet, the router has to ARP for the destination IP itself (10.1.1.5), as if it were directly connected.

The Ethernet disaster scenario: Imagine an exit-interface static for 10.1.1.0/24 on an Ethernet interface. Every packet to every unique destination in the /24 triggers an ARP request: 'Who has 10.1.1.5? 10.1.1.6? 10.1.1.7? ...' The ARP cache balloons with hundreds of entries. The local Ethernet segment floods with ARP broadcasts. CPU consumption climbs. In a production network with thousands of unique destinations, this behavior collapses performance.

Proxy ARP workaround: Some environments rely on proxy ARP on the next-hop router to answer those ARP requests on behalf of remote destinations. The next-hop router says 'I have 10.1.1.5' (even though it doesn't) and the packet flows to it, where normal routing takes over. This works but requires proxy ARP enabled, is inefficient, and is considered bad practice for routed environments.

When exit-interface-only IS appropriate:

  • Point-to-point links — Serial, PPP, HDLC, GRE tunnels, MPLS P2P. Only one possible next-hop, no ARP needed, no ambiguity. Cisco considers this the correct use case.
  • Loopback destinations — Very niche scenarios where you want traffic to hit the router's own loopback.

When NEVER to use it: Ethernet, VLAN interfaces (SVIs), any multi-access link, any tunneled interface that shares a broadcast domain. The ARP storm is always wrong.

In the routing table: S 10.1.1.0/24 is directly connected, GigabitEthernet0/0. Notice 'is directly connected' — the router literally treats the static route as if the destination were a connected network.

left.label: Exit interface on P2P (OK)
items: Only one neighbor on the line

right.label: Exit interface on Ethernet (BAD)
items: Router ARPs for EVERY destination IP
Proxy ARP hack needed or fails

hack.memory: Exit-interface-only = 'just throw it out this door' with no address. Fine on P2P (one neighbor, no MAC lookup needed). Disaster on Ethernet (router ARPs for every destination IP). Rule: if the interface is Ethernet, DON'T use exit-interface-only. Use next-hop IP or fully specified.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 FastEthernet0/0' (exit-interface-only on Ethernet). Use 'debug arp' or 'show arp' to observe ARP behavior when pinging multiple destinations in 10.1.1.0/24. The ARP cache grows with each destination. Now swap to 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 [next-hop IP]' and observe: only one ARP entry (for the next-hop). This dramatic difference cements why recursive wins on Ethernet.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 demonstrates the ARP issue explicitly. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. The exam asks 'why shouldn't you use exit-interface-only on Ethernet?' — answer: excessive ARP, one per destination. Expect this as a multiple-choice question.

3.3.f.info:
A fully specified static route names BOTH the exit interface AND the next-hop IP: ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 GigabitEthernet0/0 192.168.1.2. This is the best of both worlds — no recursive lookup (interface is already specified) AND correct ARP behavior on Ethernet (ARPs for the next-hop, not every destination).

How it works: The router skips the second recursive lookup because the exit interface is explicit. But because the next-hop IP is also present, the router ARPs for that specific next-hop (just like a recursive route) and caches the result. Every destination matching this route reuses the same ARP entry.

When to use it: Cisco recommends fully specified routes on Ethernet when you want the efficiency of a pre-resolved interface without the ARP issues of exit-interface-only. It's the 'most complete' form — no ambiguity, no performance traps. Common in production for critical routes where you want guaranteed path selection even if the RIB changes (the explicit interface locks it in).

Syntax order: Interface BEFORE next-hop. ip route [net] [mask] [interface] [next-hop]. If you reverse them, IOS usually accepts the command but may treat it differently — always interface first.

Special benefit — locking the path: If your network has multiple paths to the same next-hop IP (say, 192.168.1.2 is reachable via Gi0/0 OR Gi0/1 through different underlying networks), a recursive static might resolve differently depending on RIB state. A fully specified static pins the path explicitly — it will ALWAYS go out Gi0/0, regardless of what else is in the RIB.

Special benefit — faster convergence: Because there's no recursive lookup, the route can install and forward without waiting for the next-hop's route to be resolved. This matters at scale but is usually a non-issue on CCNA-scale networks.

In the routing table: S 10.1.1.0/24 [1/0] via 192.168.1.2, GigabitEthernet0/0. Both the next-hop and interface appear, making the forwarding decision explicit.

IPv6 equivalent: ipv6 route 2001:DB8:1::/64 GigabitEthernet0/0 2001:DB8:2::1. Same benefits apply. And for link-local next-hops, fully specified syntax is MANDATORY anyway (the rule from topic 3.3.e).

hack.memory: Fully specified = interface + next-hop together. Best of both: no recursion, no ARP flood. Syntax order: interface FIRST, then next-hop. Required form for IPv6 link-local next-hops. Use this pattern when you want maximum clarity and path locking.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 Gi0/0 192.168.1.2' on R1. Verify 'show ip route' shows BOTH the via and the interface. Check 'show arp' — one entry per next-hop, same as recursive. Now remove and re-add as exit-interface-only on the same link — observe the ARP behavior change. The fully-specified form maintains recursive-style ARP efficiency while locking the interface.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 covers all three variants with the same example. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Exam asks 'which static route form is most complete/safest on Ethernet?' — answer is fully specified. Also expect IPv6 link-local syntax questions where fully specified is the only legal form.

3.3.f.info:
Cisco's formal recommendation for static route configuration is:

  • On Ethernet / multi-access networks: Use next-hop IP (recursive) OR fully specified routes. NEVER use exit-interface-only.
  • On point-to-point links (serial, PPP, HDLC, GRE, P2P tunnels): Exit-interface-only is fine — there's only one possible next-hop and no ARP needed.

Why: Because exit-interface-only routes on Ethernet cause the router to ARP for every unique destination IP, filling the ARP cache and flooding the local segment with broadcasts. This behavior scales poorly and is considered an operational bug in production environments.

Rule of thumb hierarchy (best to worst on Ethernet):

  1. Fully specified (interface + next-hop) — most explicit, no recursion, no ARP issue.
  2. Recursive (next-hop only) — one recursive lookup, efficient ARP.
  3. Exit-interface-only — causes ARP-per-destination, avoid on Ethernet.

Rule of thumb on point-to-point:

  1. Exit-interface-only — simplest, works correctly.
  2. Fully specified — also fine, more explicit.
  3. Recursive (next-hop only) — works but requires the next-hop to be resolvable, slightly less direct.

Why this matters on the exam: The CCNA explicitly tests 'which static route type should you use on Ethernet?' The expected answer is next-hop IP or fully specified. Distractor answers include exit-interface-only, which is marked wrong because of the ARP behavior. Memorize this decision matrix.

Real-world practice: Most production configurations use recursive (next-hop IP) routes because they're the most flexible — if the underlying topology changes, the recursive lookup adapts automatically. Fully specified routes are used when you specifically want to pin the interface (path locking for critical routes). Exit-interface-only is used exclusively on P2P links where it's idiomatic.

Troubleshooting tip: If you see a network with widespread ARP storms or excessive ARP cache entries on a router, check for exit-interface-only static routes on Ethernet interfaces. This is a classic misconfiguration, especially in environments where the admin learned on serial links and didn't adapt when they switched to Ethernet.

hack.memory: Ethernet = next-hop IP or fully specified (avoid exit-interface-only). P2P = exit-interface is fine, no ARP issue. The test loves this decision matrix. If you see a static route on a Gig interface, it'd better have an IP — not just the interface name.
hack.practice: Build a Packet Tracer lab with ONE router connected to two neighbors: one via serial (P2P), one via Ethernet. Configure static routes to two remote networks — one out each interface. Use exit-interface-only on the serial (correct) and fully specified on the Ethernet (correct). Then swap: exit-interface-only on Ethernet (incorrect) and observe ARP behavior. This A/B drill makes the recommendation intuitive, not memorized.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 and Day 15. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. The exam asks this as multiple-choice: 'what's the recommended static route configuration on an Ethernet interface?' Correct answers: next-hop IP, or fully specified. Wrong answer: exit-interface-only. Guaranteed topic.

3.3.g.info:
Step 1 of static route verification is confirming the route is actually configured — the ip route or ipv6 route command exists in the running-config. This is a pure 'did I type it?' check and passes even if the route isn't currently active.

Primary commands:

  • show running-config | include ip route — lists all IPv4 static route commands in the config.
  • show running-config | include ipv6 route — IPv6 equivalent.
  • show running-config | section ip route — broader, includes surrounding context.

What this tells you: The command was entered and saved to running-config. It does NOT confirm the route is installed, working, or reachable. A configured-but-inactive route is the classic source of 'why isn't traffic flowing?' confusion.

Example output:

R1# show running-config | include ip route
ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2
ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 203.0.113.1
ip route 10.2.2.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.2.2 210

Three static routes in config: one network route, one default, one floating. The fact that they're in the output means the commands were entered. Whether they're active is a separate question — proceed to Step 2.

Common Step 1 failures:

  • The route was never typed — admin assumed it was configured but it wasn't.
  • The route was typed in the wrong mode (e.g., under an interface instead of global config) and didn't stick.
  • The route was configured and then accidentally removed by a subsequent 'no ip route ...' command.
  • The config was not saved — running-config has the route but startup-config doesn't, so after a reboot the route disappeared.

Startup vs running config: show startup-config | include ip route shows what the router will load on reboot. If running and startup differ, a 'copy run start' or 'wr mem' is needed to persist changes. This is the 'my config reverts after reboot' source.

Important: passing Step 1 is NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT. The route being configured doesn't mean it's in the routing table or usable. Always proceed to Step 2 (routing table check) before declaring success.
