hack.memory: Step 1 = 'is it typed?' Use 'show running-config | include ip route' (or ipv6 route). Fast but shallow — passing this doesn't mean the route works, only that the command exists. Then move to Step 2.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure a static route, run 'show running-config | include ip route' — see the entry. Now remove it with 'no ip route ...' and re-run the check — it's gone. Configure a second route and forget to 'copy run start,' reload the router — the route is gone again. This drill cements how Step 1 works and how it relates to startup-config.
hack.meta: Fundamental troubleshooting skill. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Expect sim questions where you have to verify config, RIB, and connectivity in the right order. Step 1 is always first.

3.3.g.info:
Step 2 of verification is checking the live routing table (RIB) to confirm the route is actually INSTALLED and ACTIVE — not just configured. A route can exist in running-config but be absent from the RIB for several reasons, so this step is non-negotiable.

Primary commands:

  • show ip route static — filters to static routes currently in the RIB.
  • show ip route — full RIB; static routes have code S (or S* for candidate default).
  • show ip route 10.1.1.0 — looks up a specific destination and shows the matching route (if any).
  • show ipv6 route static / show ipv6 route — IPv6 equivalents.

What passing means: The route appears in the table with code S and a reachable next-hop. Example: S 10.1.1.0/24 [1/0] via 192.168.1.2. The presence of this entry confirms IOS considers the route valid and is using it for forwarding.

Why a configured route might NOT be in the RIB:

  • Exit interface is down: Static route pointing out Gi0/0, but Gi0/0 is shut or the cable is unplugged. The route is suppressed until the interface comes up.
  • Next-hop unreachable: Recursive static points at 192.168.1.2, but the router has no route to 192.168.1.0/24 (interface down, subnet missing). Recursion fails, route doesn't install.
  • Higher-AD route overridden: Floating static (AD 210) is configured, but the primary (OSPF AD 110) is active — the floating static is intentionally hidden until the primary fails. Not a bug, a feature.
  • Invalid next-hop: Typo'd a next-hop that doesn't exist on any neighbor. Route won't install.

Using the specific-destination lookup: show ip route 10.1.1.50 tells the router 'show me the route you'd use for this destination.' This runs full longest-prefix-match and returns the winning route. Invaluable for confirming 'is the traffic going to use the route I expect, or a different one?'

Example output:

R1# show ip route 10.1.1.50
Routing entry for 10.1.1.0/24
  Known via "static", distance 1, metric 0
  Routing Descriptor Blocks:
  * 192.168.1.2
      Route metric is 0, traffic share count is 1

This output shows the route source (static), AD (1), metric (0), and active next-hop. The asterisk (*) marks which path is chosen when multiple equal-cost options exist.

When Step 2 fails but Step 1 passes: The route is configured but not installed. Diagnose by checking the exit interface status (show ip interface brief) and the next-hop reachability (ping the next-hop from the router).

hack.memory: Step 2 = 'is it active?' Use 'show ip route static' or 'show ip route [prefix]'. Configured ≠ installed. If the route is missing from the RIB: check interface status, check next-hop reachability, check for competing routes with lower AD. Passing Step 1 but failing Step 2 is the most common real-world issue.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer: configure 'ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.99.99' (next-hop in a non-existent subnet). Run 'show run | inc ip route' — route is there (Step 1 passes). Run 'show ip route static' — route is NOT there (Step 2 fails). Now fix the next-hop to a valid neighbor IP — both steps pass. Practice this trap/repair cycle so the distinction between 'configured' and 'installed' becomes reflexive.
hack.meta: Critical diagnostic skill. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Exam sims often show running-config and routing table together and ask 'why isn't the route installed?' — answer is usually interface down, next-hop unreachable, or floating static doing its job.

3.3.g.info:
Step 3 of verification is actually testing end-to-end connectivity with ping and traceroute. A route can be configured (Step 1) and installed in the RIB (Step 2) and still fail to deliver traffic end-to-end, because the return path may be broken.

Basic tools:

  • ping 10.1.1.5 — simple ICMP test from the router to the destination.
  • traceroute 10.1.1.5 — shows the path, hop by hop. Identifies where packets get lost.
  • ping 10.1.1.5 source 10.99.99.1 — extended ping, sources from a specific interface/IP. Critical for simulating 'traffic from the LAN' behavior.
  • ping 10.1.1.5 repeat 100 — stress test, useful for catching intermittent drops.

What passing means: Packets go to the destination AND return. Both halves of the path are working. This is the only check that confirms real-world traffic will flow.

Common Step 3 failures even when Steps 1 and 2 pass:

  • Missing return route — R1 has a route to R3's LAN, but R3 has no route back to R1's LAN. Pings from R1 reach R3 but replies are dropped. Symptom: 'Request timed out.' This is the #1 cause.
  • ACL blocking traffic — an ACL on an intermediate router denies the specific source or destination. Symptom: 'Destination unreachable' or silent drops.
  • Firewall between routers — stateful firewall drops traffic because it sees unmatched asymmetric flows.
  • Wrong mask on one end — R3's LAN has /24 but R1's route was configured as /16. Traffic goes to a wrong subnet.
  • MTU mismatch — packets get fragmented and dropped somewhere along the path. Ping with small payload works, larger payload fails.

Extended ping for diagnosing missing return routes: The extended ping with source simulates traffic from a specific subnet. Example: ping 10.3.0.5 source 10.1.0.1. This tells the destination to reply to 10.1.0.1 (a LAN address) instead of the router's transit IP. If the destination can reply to the transit IP but not the LAN IP, you've isolated the problem to a missing return route for 10.1.0.0/24 on the destination side.

Traceroute interpretation: Each line is one hop. 'Asterisks' (* * *) mean no reply from that hop — usually an intermediate router dropping or ICMP being rate-limited. If traceroute reaches the destination but returns no replies, the return path is broken. If it stops at some intermediate hop consistently, that's the broken segment.

Combined workflow: Pass Step 1 + Step 2 + Step 3 = route is fully operational. Passing any subset without passing all three = partial diagnostic information.

steps: No reply = missing return route (Step 3 FAIL)

hack.memory: Step 3 = 'does traffic actually flow?' Ping tests reachability, traceroute shows where it dies. Extended ping with 'source' catches missing return routes. 'Request timed out' = return path broken. 'Destination unreachable' = forward path broken. Different symptoms, different fixes.
hack.practice: Packet Tracer drill: R1-R2-R3 with LANs on each end. Configure forward static only (R1 knows how to reach R3's LAN, R3 doesn't know R1's LAN). From R1 run 'ping 10.3.0.5' — request times out. Run 'traceroute 10.3.0.5' — packet reaches R3 but no replies return. Add the return route on R3 — pings work. This drill makes 'missing return route' recognizable by symptom.
hack.meta: Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. The exam presents 'ping fails, here's the config, why?' scenarios. Answer is almost always missing return route, wrong mask, ACL, or interface down. Know how to use all three verification steps in sequence.

3.3.g.info:
A route being configured but not installed is the single most confusing state in static routing. The command is in running-config, show running-config | include ip route shows it, but show ip route does NOT show it — and traffic to that destination fails or takes a different path.

Why this happens — the four main causes:

  1. Exit interface is down. If the static route points out GigabitEthernet0/0 (either directly or through a recursive next-hop reachable via Gi0/0), and Gi0/0 is shut or has carrier loss, IOS pulls the route from the RIB. Check with show ip interface brief — look for 'administratively down' or 'down/down' status.
  2. Next-hop is unreachable. For a recursive static, if the router has no route to the next-hop IP, recursion fails. Example: ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.2 but there's no connected/static/dynamic route to 192.168.1.0/24 — recursion fails, the static isn't installed. Ping the next-hop from the router to confirm reachability.
  3. A lower-AD route exists for the same prefix. The static is configured but a more-trusted source (connected, OSPF with lower AD, etc.) has a competing route. Common with floating statics: primary OSPF (AD 110) installs, floating static (AD 210) stays hidden by design. Check for competing routes with show ip route [prefix].
  4. Invalid configuration. Typo in the next-hop, wrong mask, or destination address that doesn't make sense. IOS usually accepts the command anyway (because it can't validate semantics), but the route never installs.

Diagnostic sequence:

  1. Run show run | include ip route — confirm the command is there.
  2. Run show ip route static — confirm the route is NOT in the RIB.
  3. Run show ip interface brief — check if the exit interface is up/up.
  4. Ping the next-hop IP from the router. If it fails, the next-hop is unreachable.
  5. Run show ip route [next-hop IP] — confirm there's a route to the next-hop.
  6. Run show ip route [prefix] — see if a lower-AD route is winning instead.

Example scenario: Admin configures ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 Serial0/0/0. The command appears in running-config. But Serial0/0/0 is shut. The RIB doesn't show the route. Admin bring Serial0/0/0 up — route appears instantly. Admin shuts it again — route disappears again. The binding between interface state and route installation is tight.

Why this is tested: The exam loves 'given this config and this routing table, why isn't the route installed?' scenarios. You must systematically walk through the four causes — usually the answer is interface down or next-hop unreachable.

states: All checks pass → INSTALLED in RIB

hack.memory: Configured ≠ installed. If the command is in running-config but missing from RIB, walk the 4-point diagnostic: (1) interface up? (2) next-hop reachable? (3) lower-AD competitor? (4) valid syntax? The first two catch 90% of cases. Floating statics cause (3) on purpose.
hack.practice: In Packet Tracer, reproduce each failure mode: (a) configure a static pointing out an interface, then shut the interface — observe RIB drop. (b) Configure a recursive static pointing at an unreachable IP — observe RIB never installs. (c) Configure a floating static alongside OSPF — observe only OSPF appears. Fix each and watch the route return. This deliberate failure-mode practice makes 'why isn't the route installed?' a diagnosable question instead of a mystery.
hack.meta: Extremely common exam scenario. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11 covers the diagnostic sequence. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Expect a sim with a config + routing table + 'fix this' prompt — the answer is typically 'interface is down' or 'next-hop unreachable,' less often 'wrong AD on floating.'

3.3.g.info:
Static routing issues cluster into a small number of recurring patterns. Knowing these lets you diagnose most 'my routing isn't working' complaints in under a minute.

1. Missing return route (by far the #1 issue). R1 has a route to R3's LAN, but R3 doesn't have a route back to R1's LAN. Forward pings reach R3, replies get dropped. Symptom: 'Request timed out.' Fix: configure the mirror-image static on R3 pointing at R1's LAN.

2. Wrong next-hop IP. Typo in the static route's next-hop. Example: ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.255.0 192.168.1.22 but the real neighbor IP is 192.168.1.2. The route either doesn't install (if 192.168.1.22 is unreachable) or installs but traffic goes to the wrong place (if 192.168.1.22 happens to be another device). Fix: double-check next-hop IPs against show cdp neighbors detail or physical diagrams.

3. Wrong subnet mask. Configured ip route 10.1.1.0 255.255.0.0 ... (a /16) instead of 255.255.255.0 (a /24). The route covers too many or too few addresses. Traffic to unintended destinations gets steered through the wrong path. Fix: verify the mask matches the intended destination network exactly.

4. Exit interface in wrong state. Static points out Gi0/0 but Gi0/0 is 'administratively down' (shut) or 'down/down' (no carrier). Route can't install. Fix: no shutdown the interface or fix the physical layer issue.

5. Asymmetric routing. Forward path goes one way (via R1→R2→R3), return path takes a different path (via R3→R4→R1). Works for stateless traffic but breaks stateful devices (firewalls, NAT, load balancers) that expect the same flow in both directions. Fix: ensure forward and return paths are symmetric, or configure stateful devices to handle asymmetry.

6. Floating static with AD too low. 'Backup' static was configured with ip route ... 50 when the primary is OSPF (AD 110). The static wins (50 < 110), making the backup the active path. Fix: set AD to at least primary+100 (e.g., 210 for OSPF 110).

7. Forgot 'ipv6 unicast-routing.' IPv6 statics configured, routes appear in 'show ipv6 route,' but no forwarding happens. Fix: ipv6 unicast-routing in global config.

8. Wrong command syntax. ip route 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 [next-hop] (meant to be a default route but actually matches only 0.0.0.0) instead of ip route 0.0.0.0 0.0.0.0 [next-hop]. IOS accepts both but the behavior differs dramatically. Fix: verify the actual intent of each command.

9. Unsaved config. Everything works in running-config, but after reboot the router loses the static routes because 'copy run start' was never run. Fix: copy running-config startup-config after every configuration change.

Diagnostic approach: For any 'static routing broken' scenario, run the three-step verification (config → RIB → connectivity) and correlate failures to this list. Missing return route accounts for ~60% of real-world issues — always check that first.

children.name: Forward path OK but replies fail → Missing return route
Route not in RIB → Interface down or next-hop unreachable
Route in RIB but wrong path → Wrong next-hop or wrong mask
Floating static not hiding → AD too low

hack.memory: Top 3 causes: (1) missing return route, (2) next-hop unreachable, (3) exit interface down. Always check these before anything exotic. 'Request timed out' = forward OK, return broken. 'Destination unreachable' = forward broken. Know the symptom → likely cause mapping and diagnose fast.
hack.practice: Build a 'break-and-fix' Packet Tracer drill: configure a working 3-router static routing lab. Save the config. Introduce ONE fault at a time: (a) delete return route, (b) typo next-hop, (c) wrong mask, (d) shut an interface, (e) raise a floating static's AD too low. For each, run through the three-step verification and identify the cause. This targeted drill builds fast-diagnosis muscle memory.
hack.meta: Exam sims lean heavily on these common issues. Jeremy's IT Lab Day 11. Wendell Odom OCG Chapter 15. Know the symptom → cause → fix mapping for the top 5 issues cold. Under exam pressure you won't have time to reason from scratch; you need pattern recognition.
